title: Beyond Model Capability
subtitle: A System-Level Framework for AI Power

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher_unit: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center

alternate_identifiers.description: Policy brief number printed in the PDF

alternate_identifiers.identifier: Beyond Model Capability A System-Level Framework for AI Power.pdf

alternate_identifiers.description: Short form of the policy brief title
Quantifiable index structure proposed in the policy brief for assessing system-level AI power
Abbreviation for AI System Power Index
Core framework developed in the policy brief

description: This policy brief argues that AI power cannot be reduced to model capability, benchmark performance, parameter scale, or public adoption. It develops a system-level framework in which real-world AI power depends on six interacting dimensions: model capability, data quality, resource access, authority, real-world interface, and rule space. The brief introduces the AI System Power Index (ASPI) as a conceptual and partially quantifiable structure for assessing how AI systems become consequential once embedded in institutions, infrastructure, markets, public services, or security environments.
abstract: AI competition and capability are often assessed through model performance, benchmark rankings, parameter scale, public adoption, or user reach. These indicators are useful, but they capture only the visible layer of AI capability. In this brief, AI power refers to the capacity of an AI system to shape decisions, mobilize resources, influence behavior, or alter real-world outcomes within a specific institutional, operational, and rule environment. This policy brief argues that AI power depends not only on model capability, but also on data quality, resource access, authority, real-world interfaces, and rule space. A public-facing model may be highly capable but operationally constrained, while a narrower system may have greater real-world impact if it is embedded in institutions, infrastructure, financial systems, or exceptional legal, institutional, or operational environments. The central argument is that AI power is not reducible to model intelligence. It is produced by the broader system that determines what the system can know, access, mobilize, do under authorization, and change.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, systemic risk, logistics resilience, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.framework_name: AI System Power Index and System-Level AI Power Framework
methodology.source_basis: Conceptual policy analysis and framework synthesis based on existing AI measurement and governance literature, including the Stanford AI Index, the Global AI Index, the Government AI Readiness Index, and emerging work on AI power disparity. The brief develops a structural model for assessing AI power through model capability, data quality, resource access, authority, real-world interface, and rule space.
methodology.case_context: AI systems embedded in public-facing services, enterprise operations, finance, health care, critical infrastructure, defense, government administration, markets, institutional workflows, and rule-governed deployment environments.
methodology.core_problem: The central analytical problem is that public AI competition is often measured through model rankings, benchmark scores, parameter scale, and adoption, while real-world AI power depends on whether AI systems can know, access, mobilize, act under authorization, interface with operational systems, and operate within permissive or constraining rule spaces.
central_claims: Model capability is necessary but insufficient for measuring real-world AI power.
AI power is produced by a broader system connecting capability to data, resources, authority, interfaces, and rule conditions.
Public-facing models may be highly capable but operationally constrained, while narrower embedded systems may have greater real-world influence.
The AI System Power Index provides a conceptual and partially quantifiable structure for comparing system-level AI power.
The weighted geometric structure captures interaction effects because strength in one dimension cannot fully compensate for near-zero capability in another.
AI governance should move from content safety alone toward power governance: governance of access, authority, interfaces, and intervention capacity.

analytical_lenses.name: System-Level AI Power
analytical_lenses.description: Treats AI power as a compound structure generated by capability, data, resources, authority, interfaces, and rule space rather than by model intelligence alone.

analytical_lenses.name: AI System Power Index
analytical_lenses.description: Provides a partially operationalized index structure using normalized dimensions and weighted geometric aggregation.

analytical_lenses.name: Institutional Embedding
analytical_lenses.description: Examines how AI becomes consequential when placed inside decision processes, resource allocation systems, infrastructure management, or security operations.

analytical_lenses.name: Operational Consequence
analytical_lenses.description: Classifies AI systems by their ability to affect resources, services, infrastructure, institutional rights, or real-world system states.

analytical_lenses.name: Power Governance
analytical_lenses.description: Extends AI governance from public outputs and content safety to access, authorization, interfaces, intervention capacity, auditability, and accountability.

dimensions.name: Model Capability
dimensions.observed_pattern: Captures reasoning, planning, language processing, multimodal understanding, coding, prediction, simulation, adaptation, and task execution.
dimensions.interpretation: Model capability is the cognitive foundation of AI power, but it is not equivalent to AI power itself.

dimensions.name: Data Quality
dimensions.observed_pattern: Captures the value, rarity, reliability, verification level, domain specificity, and operational relevance of data available to an AI system.
dimensions.interpretation: Trusted, timely, contextual, proprietary, restricted, classified, institutional, or experience-based data can create strategic depth beyond public model rankings.

dimensions.name: Resource Access
dimensions.observed_pattern: Captures access to compute, energy, capital, infrastructure, cloud platforms, sensors, logistics networks, financial systems, robotics, human labor, and physical devices.
dimensions.interpretation: Resource access determines whether AI remains a cognitive tool or becomes an operational actor capable of material consequences.

dimensions.name: Authority
dimensions.observed_pattern: Captures the permission and mandate structure under which an AI system is allowed to act.
dimensions.interpretation: Authority determines whether capability remains constrained, expanded, delegated, or operationalized, and it shapes accountability for outcomes.

dimensions.name: Real-World Interface
dimensions.observed_pattern: Captures APIs, workflows, databases, markets, supply chains, infrastructure, physical devices, public services, legal processes, administrative platforms, and automated operational systems.
dimensions.interpretation: Interfaces convert AI from analysis into intervention by connecting it to systems whose states can be affected.

dimensions.name: Rule Space
dimensions.observed_pattern: Captures laws, regulations, institutional policies, platform rules, liability regimes, procurement rules, compliance requirements, emergency powers, national security exceptions, and gray-zone operating conditions.
dimensions.interpretation: Rule space defines how AI power is permitted, limited, expanded, normalized, or released.

key_findings: Model capability is necessary but insufficient because benchmarks, parameter scale, and public adoption measure visible performance but not real-world AI power.
Data quality is a core source of advantage, especially when data is rare, verified, proprietary, domain-specific, or experience-based.
Resource access turns capability into operational influence when AI systems connect to compute, capital, infrastructure, sensors, platforms, labor, robotics, or physical assets.
Authority determines how capability is released across public, enterprise, governmental, military, or emergency settings.
Real-world interfaces convert analytical capability into operational action by allowing AI systems to trigger actions, allocate resources, influence decisions, or alter system states.
Rule space determines how AI power is constrained or released by law, institutional policy, platform governance, national security exceptions, emergency powers, and gray-zone conditions.
The most consequential AI systems may not be the largest or most visible, but those most deeply embedded in institutions, infrastructure, markets, or rule-making environments.
AI governance should broaden from content safety to power governance, including the governance of AI access, authority, interfaces, and intervention capacity.
policy_recommendations: Map AI system access and interfaces, including data access, API connections, execution permissions, resource calls, workflow integrations, and operational-system links.
Classify AI systems by operational consequence rather than only by model size or general capability.
Apply heightened oversight to AI systems connected to financial markets, health care, public services, infrastructure, defense, or administrative decision-making.
Require clear authorization and accountability controls for high-consequence deployments.
Define human authorization chains, logging, auditability, responsibility assignment, and mechanisms for appeal, suspension, or rollback.
Treat rule-making, standards, liability rules, procurement rules, data-access regimes, export controls, national security exemptions, and platform policies as instruments that shape AI power.

watchpoints.indicator: AI systems with modest public visibility but deep integration into financial, infrastructure, defense, public-service, or administrative systems
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Identifies high-consequence AI power that may be missed by public model rankings.

watchpoints.indicator: Expansion of AI access to proprietary, restricted, classified, institutional, or real-time sensor data
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Tracks whether data quality is creating strategic advantage beyond benchmark performance.

watchpoints.indicator: Delegation of execution authority to AI systems in workflows, resource allocation, procurement, markets, or operational systems
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Assesses whether AI is shifting from advisory use to operational intervention.

watchpoints.indicator: New legal exemptions, emergency authorities, procurement rules, or platform policies enabling AI deployment in high-consequence settings
watchpoints.analytical_purpose: Monitors rule-space changes that may release or constrain AI power.

tables.title: Six Dimensions of AI Power
tables.description: Summarizes the six dimensions of AI power: model capability, data quality, resource access, authority, real-world interface, and rule space, with each dimension linked to a core question and policy relevance.
tables.source: Author’s conceptual synthesis based on existing AI measurement frameworks and the proposed system-level AI power framework.
tables.note: The table is conceptual and policy-analytic rather than a validated measurement instrument.

disclaimer: This policy brief is an institutional publication of EPINOVA, prepared by Dr. Shaoyuan Wu in his capacity as Director of the Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC. The analysis is based on publicly available information and scenario-based analytical estimates and does not represent the official position of any government. The publication is intended solely for research and policy discussion purposes and does not constitute legal, military, operational, or sanctions-compliance advice.
